entry.title: Re: Print Handling Unit Label During Creation of Inbound...
entry.content:
Dear @giorgio_mazzeo7,

The minimum standard test setup in Output Parameter Determination for this test should be maintained as follows:

1. Show Rules For = Handling Unit

2. In Output Type, maintain HU_LABEL with Dispatch Time for example = 1 (Immediately)

3. In Channel, maintain PRINT for HU_LABEL

 

4. In Printer Settings, maintain a valid Print Queue for example DEFAULT if this queue exists and is active in the tenant and Number of Copies = 1 for HU_LABEL

 

5. In Form Template, maintain HU_LABEL for Channel = PRINT

After maintaining these steps the rules should be saved, checked and activated and the process should then be retested by creating the inbound delivery in the scenario where handling units are created automatically from packing instructions. SAP documents that automatic printing of HU labels is supported for HUs created during automatic packing in inbound delivery creation

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/571fc3d95d564f55b9ed18f581bf3567.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/8a3d957636c245ab9e4de3c2285888b7.html

If the label still does not print after this setup the next checks should be outside the basic OPD rule itself. First, whether automatic printing of HU labels for processes is configured for the inbound process and second, whether the selected print queue is correctly maintained and actually receives print jobs in Maintain Print Queues. If both are correct and the issue is still reproducible the next defensible step would be to raise an SAP incident

Summary:

The minimum standard setup for this test is Handling Unit + HU_LABEL + Dispatch Time Immediately + PRINT + valid print queue + HU_LABEL form template. If the issue remains after this the next checks are the process-specific automatic printing configuration and the print queue, rather than the basic OPD structure itself

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Currency Conversion Made Easy with SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
I'd love to add a perspective from a slightly different angle - not to say this approach is wrong (it clearly works!), but to spark a thought: what if we didn't need most of those steps at all?

When I look at the iFlow you've built, I see something really interesting: almost every step - the JSON-to-XML conversion, the XSLT mapping, the Groovy validation, the HTML formatting for the email - those aren't really business logic. They exist because the systems involved don't speak the same language, and the integration layer has to translate between them on every single request.

That's what I'd call the synchronous integration tax. And it's very real.

Now imagine flipping the architecture around with SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM):

Instead of calling an external exchange rate API per request, a single lightweight publisher fetches the rate and drops it onto an event topic - something like fx/rates/EUR/USD. From that moment on, any system that needs that rate just subscribes. Treasury, procurement, invoicing, reporting - they all get it instantly. One fetch, unlimited consumers, zero duplication.

And here's the part I find most exciting: the mapping steps disappear. Not because we got better at mapping - but because we removed the reason mapping was needed in the first place. When you define a clean canonical event schema once (e.g. a simple CloudEvents-formatted JSON with from, to, rate, timestamp), every producer and consumer agrees on the shape of the data upfront. There's no JSON↔XML conversion in the middle, no XSLT to generate HTML, no Groovy to validate what the schema registry already enforces at publish time.

And the email output - totally understandable for a demo! But in a real scenario, that converted rate is data in motion. It belongs in a system, not an inbox. A dashboard subscribes to the topic and shows it live. An AI agent subscribes and decides whether to trigger a hedging action. Nobody waits for an email.

To summarize in one sentence: SAP Integration Suite connects A to B. SAP Advanced Event Mesh connects A to everything - in real time, at scale, without anyone asking.

I just couldn't resist adding the EDA twist! 😊 Would love to hear your thoughts - have you worked with AEM or event-driven patterns before?

entry.title: Re: GitHub Copilot for SAP ABAP in VS Code: Setup Guide for On-Premise Teams
entry.content:
Great article! I’m a contributor to ABAP FS and have a few pointers:

1. It’s recommended to always use Agent mode with ABAP FS. Ask mode cannot use the tools that ABAP FS provides which means Copilot can’t access your SAP system and will only depend on the knowledge it was trained on.

2. I think vs code is about to deprecate Edit mode. It’ll only be Ask and Agent soon

3. Copilot can also create objects (you choose the TR for non-local objects)

4. I recommend skills over custom instructions as they’re hot-loaded when needed as opposed to instructions files which are always loaded, even when it’s not needed.
ABAP FS can do a lot more - subagents, skills, heartbeats, SAP Data Notebooks, Debugging with replay, dependency graphs, cross system object comparison etc., 

I recommend going through the walkthroughs added in the latest version to get an idea of what else it can do. Feedback welcome!!

entry.title: Re: Step-by-Step Guide: Real Time Currency Conversion using SAP Integration Suite (CPI)
entry.content:
I want to add a friendly "but what if..." to this, because I think it opens up a really interesting architectural discussion.

Everything you've built here is solid integration engineering - but when I read through the steps, I notice something: a lot of the complexity comes not from the currency conversion itself, but from the way the systems are wired together. The HTTPS trigger from Postman, the synchronous API call to RapidAPI, the content modifiers to extract and pass parameters, the JSON-to-XML transformation, the XSLT for output formatting - none of that is really business logic. It's all translation overhead that exists because each component speaks a slightly different language and we're mediating between them in real time, on every single request.

What if we stepped back and asked: does this actually need to be request-driven?

With SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM), the architecture flips completely. Instead of asking for an exchange rate every time someone needs one, a single publisher fetches the rate from RapidAPI on a schedule - or whenever the rate changes - and publishes it as an event. Any system that needs EUR→USD just subscribes to that topic. No per-request API calls. No caller waiting for a response chain to complete. No email as the delivery mechanism.

And the beautiful side effect? Most of those iFlow steps simply vanish. The JSON↔XML conversion disappears because consumers read the event in whatever format they need natively. The XSLT for HTML formatting disappears because no one is emailing a table - a dashboard or downstream system consumes the event directly. The Groovy validation disappears because the schema registry enforces event structure at publish time, before anything enters the mesh.

The exception handling you built - which I genuinely appreciate - becomes even simpler too. Instead of catching failures mid-flow, the broker handles guaranteed delivery and replay. Your publisher retries independently, and subscribers always get the last valid rate even if the source API had a blip.

I know this shifts the thinking quite a bit from iFlow-first to event-first - but that's exactly the shift that makes integrations scalable, resilient, and honestly much less noisy to maintain.

Would love to know: is this for a learning exercise, or are you building toward something production-like? That might change how far down the EDA rabbit hole is worth going! 😊

entry.title: Stop Calling APIs for Every Currency Conversion - with BTP Advanced Event Mesh
entry.content:
If you've been spending time on the SAP Community lately, you may have noticed a little trend: several really well-written blog posts showing how to build currency conversion flows in SAP Integration Suite. They're solid learning examples - Postman sends a JSON payload, an iFlow calls an external exchange rate API, some transformations happen, and the result arrives in a nicely formatted email.

I love seeing this kind of hands-on content. And I want to add a friendly "but wait..." - because I think there's a fundamentally different way to think about this problem. One that removes most of the complexity instead of elegantly managing it.

Let me show you what currency conversion looks like through an Event-Driven Architecture lens, using SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM).

What the iFlow approach is really doing

Let's be honest about the architecture behind those integration flows. Here's the typical pattern:

  1. A caller (Postman, an app, a system) sends a request: "give me EUR to USD"
  2. The iFlow wakes up, validates the input, calls an external API (RapidAPI, ECB, etc.)
  3. The result goes through a series of transformations: JSON → XML → XSLT → HTML
  4. The converted amount lands in someone's inbox as a formatted email

Every single step in that chain exists for one reason: the systems involved don't speak the same language, and the integration layer mediates between them - synchronously, on every request.

That's not a bug. That's what Integration Suite was designed for. But it does come with a cost.

The synchronous integration tax

Look at those iFlow steps again - not from an implementation perspective, but from a business logic perspective:

  • Groovy script to validate JSON → not business logic. It's a workaround for the fact that there's no contract between caller and receiver.
  • Content modifier to extract parameters → not business logic. It's format negotiation.
  • JSON-to-XML conversion → definitely not business logic. Pure translation overhead.
  • XSLT to generate HTML → presentation logic that has no business being in a middleware layer.
  • Mail adapter → the result is delivered to a human inbox, which is a dead end for any downstream system that might also need that rate.

None of this is wrong. But every single one of these steps is a symptom of tight coupling - and tight coupling is exactly what we're trying to move away from in modern integration architecture.

The event-driven alternative

Now let's rethink this from scratch with AEM.

The key question is: does currency conversion actually need to be request-driven?

Exchange rates don't change because someone asked for them. They change because the market moves. So why are we building an architecture that fetches them on demand - potentially hundreds of times a day, from multiple systems - when we could publish them once and let every interested party receive them instantly?

Here's what the event-driven approach looks like:

Step 1: A single Rate Publisher

A lightweight service (a Cloud Foundry app, a BTP function, even a scheduled job) fetches the latest exchange rates from your source API on a schedule - or reacts to rate-change webhooks if the provider supports them. It then publishes a structured event to AEM:

json
{
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "type": "fx.rate.updated",
  "source": "ecb.europa.eu",
  "time": "2025-04-09T08:00:00Z",
  "data": {
    "from": "EUR",
    "to": "USD",
    "rate": 1.082,
    "validFrom": "2025-04-09T00:00:00Z"
  }
}

Published to a topic: fx/rates/EUR/USD

That's it. One publish. One API call.

Step 2: Every consumer subscribes

Any system that needs exchange rates - invoicing, procurement, treasury, reporting, AI agents - subscribes to fx/rates/> (the wildcard catches all currency pairs). They receive the event the moment it's published. No polling. No HTTP round trips. No waiting.

Your SAP S/4HANA system gets it. Your custom finance app gets it. Your AI agent gets it. All simultaneously. All from the same single upstream API call.

Step 3: Consumers own their format

Here's where the mapping steps disappear - not because we got better at mapping, but because we removed the reason mapping was needed in the first place.

When you define a clean event schema once and every participant agrees on it upfront, there's nothing to translate in the middle. A Java consumer reads JSON natively. An ABAP consumer does its own deserialization. A Python AI agent processes the event directly. The broker doesn't care about format - and neither does the publisher.

No JSON-to-XML. No XSLT. No Groovy validation. Those steps were artifacts of tight coupling, and tight coupling is gone.

But what about reliability?

The iFlow approach has one hidden fragility: if the external exchange rate API is down, the integration fails - and every caller gets an error. You can build retry logic into the iFlow, but it only helps the current caller.

With AEM:

  • The Rate Publisher handles retries independently of any consumer
  • AEM supports guaranteed message delivery - if a consumer is temporarily offline, it receives the event when it comes back
  • Last-value retained messages mean a new subscriber always receives the most recent valid rate immediately on connect, even if no new rate has been published since it started

Your invoicing system reconnects after a maintenance window at 2 AM? It gets the latest rate instantly. No missed events, no stale data.

And what about the email?

The email output in those iFlow demos is charming - and totally understandable for a learning exercise. But think about what it represents: a conversion result delivered to a human inbox, where it stops being useful to any downstream system.

In an event-driven world, the converted amount is data in motion. It doesn't go to an inbox - it feeds back into the mesh. A treasury dashboard subscribes to consumption events and visualizes rates in real time. An AI agent subscribes and evaluates whether a rate threshold should trigger a hedging action. A reporting service aggregates daily rates without ever making an API call.

The email is the end of a pipeline. The event is the beginning of an ecosystem.

Putting it together: Sense → Think → Act

This is where it gets really exciting - especially when you bring agentic AI into the picture.

With AEM as the backbone, currency rate data flows through a Sense → Think → Act loop:

  • Sense: AEM receives rate update events from the publisher and distributes them across all connected systems
  • Think: An AI agent (via a SAP Agent or any LLM-powered service) evaluates the rate in the context of open purchase orders, hedging thresholds, or FX exposure — and decides whether action is needed
  • Act: The agent publishes a command event — trigger a hedge, create an FX booking, send a targeted alert — without any human in the loop

This is not something you can bolt onto an iFlow. It requires a fundamentally different architecture: one where data flows continuously, systems are decoupled, and intelligence can be inserted at any point in the mesh.

The bottom line

  Integration Suite iFlow SAP Advanced Event Mesh
API calls per rateOne per requestOne per rate change
Consumers servedOne callerUnlimited subscribers
Mapping stepsSeveral (JSON↔XML, XSLT, etc.)None — schema defined once
Failure impactCaller gets errorPublisher retries independently
New consumer addedNew iFlow or modificationJust subscribe
AI/agent readyNoYes — natively

The iFlow currency conversion demos on this community are great teaching material — they genuinely are. But when you're thinking about production architecture, ask yourself: am I solving the problem, or am I solving the problem of not having the right foundation?

SAP Advanced Event Mesh is that foundation. And once it's in place, "currency conversion" stops being an integration project and becomes a single subscription.

Happy connecting — the event-driven way! 🚀

entry.title: Using an Unbundled Apache Tomcat - What is Your configuration?
entry.content:
In our organization we are NOT using the bundled Apache Tomcat that comes with the SAP BusinessObjects install.  We are running our own separate Apache Tomcat installation so we can upgrade it independently from SAP BusinessObjects.

I am curious if anyone else is doing this and, if you are, what are the specifications for your Apache Tomcat configuration?  We mainly have configured it to secure communications to HTTPS only.

I ask because I have a few tickets open with SAP regarding 404 errors that are caused by a double slash "//" in the URL path sent by the BusinessObjects software components loaded in the browser.  It seems (based on Internet searching) Apache Tomcat was changed somewhere along the version 9 trunk to no longer automatically convert a double slash "//" to a single slash "/".  Therefore, my separate installation is triggering 404 errors for simple things such as a couple of GIF images and for a couple of JavaScript files in the CMC.  Nothing major but I am working with SAP to have them fix the calls with double slashes "//".

Any thoughts anyone?  Are you noticing this as well and/or have you tweaked the Apache Tomcat configuration to address this?  I do not have any information (yet) from SAP indicating whether or not the bundled Apache Tomcat is configured to allow double slash conversion to single slash in URLs automatically.

Thanks very much for your time.  😁

entry.title: Re: SAP Integration Suite - Inbuilt Retry option for HTTP Receiver Adapter
entry.content:
Unfortunately, if the target system’s “Retry-After” interval is larger than 60 seconds (looking at you Ariba), CPI gives up and throws an error on first try. Sigh.

“Error occurred while processing the Retry-After header: Retry-After value in seconds is :300, Max Retry-After value that is supported is 60 sec, Hence Retry is skipped, for the original exception HTTP operation”

entry.title: Re: Issue in Generating Intercompany Billing Request – Er...
entry.content:
Dear @ZakariaELMartaoui,

Thank you for the clarification.

Material-related setup can be relevant in this process but SAP documents that under a different error pattern for example Material was not determined for journal entry, which points to missing or incomplete material determination in intercompany billing profile ICOBLG001.

In your case the screenshot shows a different message: There are no postings. Cannot create Billing Document Request. SAP documents this error specifically for Generate Intercompany Billing Request and explains that it occurs when the billing run does not find supported billable postings for the new solution, or when the related G/L accounts are not mapped correctly in cost element group YBPS_BILLI. SAP also states that these G/L accounts must not be revenue accounts so therefore, please verify the below SAP Notes and SAP Help link again until you receive a response from SAP Support:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3553231

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/29cf986299714386847f4d4c9bb86994/b12b53a60e364d83a631941af0f53c39.html

https://me.sap.com/notes/3399540

Best Regards,

Dawid

This is the SAP Developer
News for April 9th, 2026.

[DJ]
The number one developer event annually is coming up soon in the SAP calendar. That's Code Connect. It's in July, but the tickets for all the events within Code Connect go on sale on the 16th of April. That's coming up very soon. so that's tickets for the CodeJams and also the individual conferences so there are four CodeJams all together OpenUI5, CAP AI Agents and HANA AI and there's UI5con of course reCAP and HANA Tech Con, three individual one-day conferences as well each event is ticketed all the tickets are free, and the tickets go on sale, quote-unquote, 16th April. See you there.

[Rich, Thiago, Alessandra, Bruno and Antonia]
Hey, friends. Rich Heilman here, and I'm joined by Thomas Young behind the camera. And we're here in Sao Paulo, Brazil at the SAP offices, and we're doing an SAP CodJam today on Joule for Developers, ABAP Developers, having a lot of fun getting through the exercises. Once we go back, I'll show them some more fun stuff. And yeah, it's been a great time so far. And here's the rest of my friends from Brazil. What's up folks! There you are in the second part of the CodeJam Roadshow here in Brazil and Ale talks a little more. So, yesterday and today we are in São Paulo and tomorrow we will be in Londrina, Paraná. And in the coming days as well ... This Friday we're going to have two simultaneous events, one in Curitiba and the other in Ribeirão Preto. And after that, we will meet on Saturday in Campinas for the SAP Inside Track. Come join us.

[Tom]
Friend of the show, Oliver Graeff, recently posted about an upcoming free webinar called The Art and Science of SAPUI5 in SAP Build. This webinar on April the 15th, which we've offered twice that day to accommodate more time zones, We'll cover information about how to develop SAPUI5, Fiori, and all things UI inside the SAP Build environment. So if you're interested, check out the registration information and all the other details in the show notes. And while we're on the subject of user interfaces, Oliver also posted about recent updates to the UI5 and Fiori Roadmap Explorer. So what a better way to prepare for the upcoming webinar than to check out all the latest that's been published in the Roadmap Explorer.

[DJ]
We just wanted to let you know that there's a recent new series an expert session series over on the SAP Developers YouTube channel the topic is CAP Node.js and we have special guest Daniel Schlachter from the CAP enablement team there are three episodes so far you can watch the replays of those and subscribe to get notifications for the upcoming episodes in this series. See you online Fridays at the usual time in the chat for these expert session Premieres.

[Nora]
Did you know that you can use Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core to build state-of-the-art AI agents with LangGraph? Now the cool thing is that AI Foundation has a lot of integrations with langchain. Now I'm going to show you how that works using the Python SDK. So up here you can see that I'm using the create_react_agent which is a pre-built AI agent from LangGraph and instead of using LangGraph to initialize the models as well, I'm going to use the Gen AI Hub SDK to initialize a model in a way that the LangGraph agent is going to understand it. So now over here you can see that I am here initializing my model. You can use any of the over 40 models that we have available in Generative AI Hub to power your agent here. So this is going to be your agent's brain, right? And then down here, when I actually initialize the actual create_react_agent from LangGraph, I'm going to hand over the LLM that I just initialized using Generative AI Hub. And then you can hand over any tools that you like. So here I'm, for example, using Playwright browser tools so that my agent can access the browser. But you can really use any tools you want and add them to your LangGraph agent here and try it out.

entry.title: Re: Bank Keys
entry.content:
Dear @Simon_89,

For Public Cloud the relevant configuration activity is SSCUI 102592 Set Country-Specific Checks

This is the standard SAP place to review the country-specific rule that governs bank-related checks, including how the Bank Key is expected for a given country/region. SAP Help also explicitly references this SSCUI in the context of bank validation. For your migration the recommended approach is to review SSCUI 102592 for each country in scope before loading Bank Master Data and align the migrated Bank Key values to the country-specific rule maintained there. On the SAP Help Portal, you can find references and examples for this configuration but the operational rule to be applied in your project should be confirmed directly in the SSCUI for each country included in the migration scope

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4977e7737cc747edb51d806619e3d146/dde821db53d54a95a04adae80cb7c79c.html

Also, Bank is a standard migration object in Public Cloud so this validation should be treated as part of the standard migration preparation for bank master data

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/85233b50252f4e729ea46feff4874629.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/20607b6de19a4d34a41b0abec6186b01.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/f32db0c240484241abc53a876253e118.html

Summary:

Use in CBC SSCUI 102592 as the reference point for the country-specific Bank Key rule. SAP Help confirms this activity, while the final country-by-country validation should be checked directly in the SSCUI before migration

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: XML Annotations: The Essentials Before Adapting Documentation Samples to Your Project
entry.content:
The Problem

When building Fiori Elements apps, you sometimes need to add XML annotations on the UI side. The usual approach is to search for examples in the UI5 Demo Kit. For instance, you might find something like this:

Disabling the Search filter

<Annotations Target="SAP__self.Container/SalesOrder">
   <Annotation Term="SAP__capabilities.SearchRestrictions">
      <Record>
         <PropertyValue Property="Searchable" Bool="false" />
      </Record>
   </Annotation>
</Annotations>

However, simply copying this into your annotation.xml won't work. To figure out what needs to be changed, you need to understand the basic structure of annotations.

 

What You Need to Know

1. Annotation Targets

The target of an annotation varies depending on the annotation you want to apply. Here are the most common ones:

TargetSyntax for Target attributeExample in UI5 Documentation
EntityType{Namespace}.{EntityType}Common for UI annotations (UI.FieldGroup, UI.HeaderInfo, etc.). Note: documentation examples often show only the inner <Annotation> element, omitting the outer <Annotations Target="..."> wrapper.
EntitySet{Namespace}.{EntityContainer}/{EntitySet}TravelService.EntityContainer/Travel [1]
Property{Namespace}.{EntityType}/{Property}Z0020_CDS.Z0020Type/CountryID [3]
NavigationProperty{Namespace}.{EntityContainer}/{EntitySet}/{NavigationProperty}myService.EntityContainer/SalesOrderManage/_Item [2]
Action (Bound){Namespace}.{Action}({BindingParameterType})com.c_salesordermanage_sd.CreateWithSalesOrderType(com.c_salesordermanage_sd.SalesOrderManage) [2]
Action (Unbound){Namespace}.{Action}SAP__self.CreateWithSalesOrderType [2]
Action Parameter{Namespace}.{Action}/{Parameter}SalesOrder.CreateWithSalesOrderType/SalesOrganization [2]
EntityContainer{Namespace}.{EntityContainer}namespace.EntityContainer [4]

[1] Disabling the Editing Status Filter
[2] Actions
[3] In / Out Mappings in the ValueList Annotation
[4] Adapting the Filter Bar

The Namespace, EntityType, EntitySet, and other names used in the Target attribute can all be found in your service's metadata.xml document. When developing a Fiori Elements app, this file is typically located in your project folder.

CAP Example

In a CAP service, open metadata.xml and look for the following:

<Schema Namespace="QuoteService" ...>  <!-- Namespace -->

    <EntityContainer Name="EntityContainer">  <!-- EntityContainer name -->
        <EntitySet Name="Quotations" EntityType="QuoteService.Quotations">  <!-- EntitySet name -->
            <NavigationPropertyBinding Path="attachments" Target="Quotations_attachments"/>
        </EntitySet>
    </EntityContainer>

    <EntityType Name="Quotations">  <!-- EntityType name -->
        <Property Name="Description" Type="Edm.String"/>  <!-- Property name -->
        <NavigationProperty Name="attachments" .../>  <!-- NavigationProperty name -->
    </EntityType>

</Schema>

From this, you can construct your target values:

TargetValue
EntityTypeQuoteService.Quotations
EntitySetQuoteService.EntityContainer/Quotations
PropertyQuoteService.Quotations/Description
NavigationPropertyQuoteService.Quotations/attachments
EntityContainerQuoteService.EntityContainer

RAP Example

In a RAP service, the structure is similar, but notice the Alias attribute on the Schema element:

<Schema Namespace="com.sap.gateway.srvd.dmo.flight_r.v0001" Alias="SAP__self">  <!-- Namespace & Alias -->

    <EntityContainer Name="Container">  <!-- EntityContainer name -->
        <EntitySet Name="Connection" EntityType="...ConnectionType">
            <NavigationPropertyBinding Path="_Flight" Target="Flight"/>
        </EntitySet>
    </EntityContainer>

    <EntityType Name="ConnectionType">  <!-- EntityType name -->
        <Property Name="AirlineID" Type="Edm.String"/>  <!-- Property name -->
        <NavigationProperty Name="_Flight" Type="Collection(...FlightType)"/>  <!-- NavigationProperty name -->
    </EntityType>

</Schema>

In RAP, the Target attribute uses the Alias (SAP__self) instead of the full Namespace. The Alias is always SAP__self in RAP services, so you can use it as-is.

TargetValue
EntityTypeSAP__self.FlightType
EntitySetSAP__self.Container/Flight
PropertySAP__self.FlightType/AirlineID
NavigationPropertySAP__self.ConnectionType/_Flight
EntityContainerSAP__self.Container

Note: The UI5 documentation does not indicate whether an annotation example is based on CAP or RAP. This can be a source of confusion — for example, SAP__self and Container in the Target attribute are RAP conventions, while CAP uses the full Namespace and EntityContainer. Always check your own service's metadata.xml to determine the correct values.

 

2. Annotations require a Reference definition

This is why simply copying a snippet from the documentation into your annotation.xml won't work.

For example, to use the annotation shown at the beginning of this post, you need to add the following Reference:

<edmx:Edmx xmlns:edmx="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edmx" Version="4.0">
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://oasis-tcs.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Org.OData.Capabilities.V1.xml">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="Org.OData.Capabilities.V1" Alias="Capabilities"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    ...
</edmx:Edmx>

OData vocabularies used in Fiori Elements are defined in two locations:

  • sap.github.io: https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/
  • oasis-tcs.github.io: https://oasis-tcs.github.io/odata-vocabularies/

Here are the vocabularies you will encounter most often when working with Fiori Elements.

SAP vocabularies

AliasNamespaceURI
UIcom.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/UI.xml
Commoncom.sap.vocabularies.Common.v1https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Common.xml
HTML5com.sap.vocabularies.HTML5.v1https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/HTML5.xml

OASIS vocabularies

AliasNamespaceURI
CoreOrg.OData.Core.V1https://oasis-tcs.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Org.OData.Core.V1.xml
CapabilitiesOrg.OData.Capabilities.V1https://oasis-tcs.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Org.OData.Capabilities.V1.xml

Tip: If you want to dig deeper, the vocabulary specification files are available at the URIs listed above. Each annotation term definition includes an AppliesTo attribute, which tells you exactly which target types the annotation can be applied to.

For example, in Org.OData.Capabilities.V1.xml:

<Term Name="SearchRestrictions" Type="Capabilities.SearchRestrictionsType" Nullable="false" AppliesTo="EntitySet Collection">
<Annotation Term="Core.AppliesViaContainer"/>
<Annotation Term="Core.Description" String="Restrictions on search expressions"/>
</Term>

This tells you that SearchRestrictions can be applied to an EntitySet target.

 

Step-by-step: Applying a Documentation Sample to Your annotation.xml

Let's walk through how to actually apply the following annotation — the same one from the beginning of this post — to your own project.

<Annotations Target="SAP__self.Container/SalesOrder">
   <Annotation Term="SAP__capabilities.SearchRestrictions">
      <Record>
         <PropertyValue Property="Searchable" Bool="false" />
      </Record>
   </Annotation>
</Annotations>

 

Step 1: Understand the annotation target

Target="SAP__self.Container/SalesOrder"

This follows the format {Namespace or Alias}.{EntityContainer}/{EntitySet}.

You can look up the exact values in your service's metadata.xml:

  • Replace SAP__self with your service's Namespace (CAP) or keep it as-is (RAP)
  • Replace Container with your EntityContainer name
  • Replace SalesOrder with your EntitySet name

Result:

<Annotations Target="MyService.EntityContainer/MyEntity">
   <Annotation Term="SAP__capabilities.SearchRestrictions">
      <Record>
         <PropertyValue Property="Searchable" Bool="false" />
      </Record>
   </Annotation>
</Annotations>

 

Step 2: Add the Reference

The term SAP__capabilities tells you this is a Capabilities vocabulary annotation. Add the following Reference inside the root <edmx:Edmx> element of your annotation.xml, before the <Annotations> block:

<edmx:Edmx xmlns:edmx="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edmx" Version="4.0">

    <!-- Add the Reference here -->
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://oasis-tcs.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Org.OData.Capabilities.V1.xml">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="Org.OData.Capabilities.V1" Alias="Capabilities"/>
    </edmx:Reference>

    <!-- Your annotations go below -->
    <Annotations Target="...">
        ...
    </Annotations>

</edmx:Edmx>

Also, replace the SAP__capabilities prefix in the annotation term with the Alias you defined in the Reference. In this case, SAP__capabilities becomes Capabilities. The final annotation will look like this:

<edmx:Edmx xmlns:edmx="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edmx" Version="4.0">
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://oasis-tcs.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Org.OData.Capabilities.V1.xml">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="Org.OData.Capabilities.V1" Alias="Capabilities"/>
    </edmx:Reference>

    <Annotations Target="MyService.EntityContainer/MyEntity">
        <Annotation Term="Capabilities.SearchRestrictions">
            <Record>
                <PropertyValue Property="Searchable" Bool="false" />
            </Record>
        </Annotation>
    </Annotations>
</edmx:Edmx>

 

Conclusion

XML annotations can feel intimidating at first, but there are really just two things you need to get right before applying any annotation from the documentation to your project:

  1. The Target — identify your Namespace, EntityContainer, EntitySet, and other names from your service's metadata.xml file
  2. The Reference — add the correct edmx:Reference for the vocabulary you are using

Once you have these two in place, any annotation snippet you find in the UI5 documentation can be adapted to your project. Happy annotating!

entry.title: Custom Sorting for Virtual Elements in RAP
entry.content:
Introduction

In the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP), virtual elements are essential for displaying computed data that doesn't exist in the database—such as deriving a month or year from a timestamp. However, because these elements are calculated at runtime, they lack standard OData capabilities like server-side sorting and filtering. In this article, we will explore how to overcome these limitations by implementing a SADL exit. We’ll walk through how to enable full sorting and calculation capabilities for virtual elements, ensuring a seamless experience for the end user.

What are Virtual Elements in RAP?

In the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP), a virtual element is a field defined in a CDS (Core Data Services) projection view that does not have a corresponding column in the underlying database table.

Real-Time Business Scenario: Sales Period Reporting

In many SAP implementations, the business requires reports segmented by Month and Year (e.g., for Monthly Sales Performance). While the underlying database table stores a precise (Created On) date, it does not store the Month or Year as separate columns.

The Problem

If we create a Fiori Elements app and want to show "Sales Month" and "Sales Year" as separate columns:

  1. Simple Virtual Elements can calculate these strings (e.g., "January", "2024").

  2. The Failure: When a user tries to sort the "Sales Month" column, the UI will throw an error or do nothing because the OData service doesn't know how to sort a field that doesn't exist in the database.

The Solution

By using a SADL Exit, we "map" the virtual Month/Year fields back to the physical Sales date field. This allows the database to perform the sort on the actual date, while the user sees the sorted Month or Year on their screen.

Implementation Steps

Step 1: Create a database table

Define the physical storage. This is where the raw data lives before any calculations happen.

@EndUserText.label : 'Sales table'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zsales_table {

  key client      : abap.clnt not null;
  key sales_uuid  : sysuuid_x16 not null;
  sales_id        : abap.char(10);
  sales_date      : abap.dats;
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zsales_table.currency'
  total_amount    : abap.curr(15,2);
  currency        : abap.cuky;
  last_changed_at : timestampl;

}

Step 2: Create a interface view on top of database table.

This layer projects the database fields into a clean, business-oriented structure.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Interface view for sales table'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define root view entity zi_sales_table
  as select from zsales_table
{
  key sales_uuid      as SalesUuid,
      sales_id        as SalesId,
      sales_date      as SalesDate,
      @Semantics.amount. currencyCode: 'Currency'
      total_amount    as TotalAmount,
      currency        as Currency,
      last_changed_at as LastChangedAt
}

 Step 3: Create a Consumption view

This is where the Virtual Elements are defined. We use annotations to link the UI to the specific ABAP logic for calculation and sorting.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Projection view for sales table'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

define root view entity ZP_SALESPROJ
  provider contract transactional_query
  as projection on zi_sales_table
{
      .facet: [{ type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
                    position: 10,
                    label: 'Salestable',
                    targetQualifier: 'GENERAL',
                    purpose: #STANDARD }]
                    
      .lineItem: [{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Uuid' }]
  key SalesUuid,

      .lineItem: [{ position: 20, label: 'Sales Id' }]
      .identification: [{ position: 20, label: 'Sales Id' }]
      SalesId,

      .lineItem: [{ position: 30, label: 'Sales order date' }]
      .identification: [{ position: 30, label: 'Sales order date', qualifier: 'GENERAL' }]
      SalesDate,

      .lineItem: [{ position: 40, label: 'Sales order year' }]
      .identification: [{ position: 40, label: 'Sales order year', qualifier: 'GENERAL' }]
      @ObjectModel.virtualElement: true
      @ObjectModel.virtualElementCalculatedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_SALES_VE_HANDLER'
      @ObjectModel.sort.transformedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_SALES_VE_HANDLER'
  virtual SalesYear  : abap.char(4),

      .lineItem: [{ position: 50, label: 'Sales order month' }]
      .identification: [{ position: 50, label: 'Sales order month' , qualifier: 'GENERAL' }]
      @ObjectModel.virtualElement: true
      @ObjectModel.virtualElementCalculatedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_SALES_VE_HANDLER'
      @ObjectModel.sort.transformedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_SALES_VE_HANDLER'
  virtual SalesMonth : abap.char(2),

      .lineItem: [{ position: 60, label: 'Total amount' }]
      .identification: [{ position: 60, label: 'Total amount' }]
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency'
      TotalAmount,

      Currency
}

Step 5: The Handler Class Logic

This class implements two critical interfaces: if_sadl_exit_calc_element_read for the calculation and if_sadl_exit_sort_transform  to enable sorting.

CLASS zcl_sales_ve_handler DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    INTERFACES if_sadl_exit_calc_element_read.
    INTERFACES if_sadl_exit_sort_transform.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.



CLASS zcl_sales_ve_handler IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_sadl_exit_calc_element_read~get_calculation_info.
    IF line_exists( it_requested_calc_elements[ table_line = 'SALESYEAR' ] ) OR
           line_exists( it_requested_calc_elements[ table_line = 'SALESMONTH' ] ).
      APPEND 'SALESDATE' TO et_requested_orig_elements.

    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD if_sadl_exit_calc_element_read~calculate.
    DATA lt_data TYPE TABLE OF zp_salesproj WITH DEFAULT KEY.
    lt_data = CORRESPONDING #( it_original_data ).


    LOOP AT lt_data ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_row>).
      <fs_row>-SALESYEAR  = <fs_row>-salesdate(4).
      <fs_row>-SALESMONTH = <fs_row>-salesdate+4(2).
    ENDLOOP.
    ct_calculated_data = CORRESPONDING #( lt_data ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD if_sadl_exit_sort_transform~map_element.
    CASE iv_element.
      WHEN 'SALESYEAR' OR 'SALESMONTH'.
        " Map the UI sort request to the database field 'SALESDATE'
        APPEND VALUE #( name = 'SALESDATE' ) TO et_sort_elements.
    ENDCASE.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

Result:


With the handler class implemented, the Sales order month and Sales order year ( Virtual Elements) now behaves like a standard database fields.

entry.title: Season 10 of Let's Talk Data Podcast Series: A focus on the data professionals that make it happen
entry.content:
Season 10 of SAP Let’s Talk Data is built for practitioners who make data work. We’re shifting from high-level storytelling to hard-earned, technical lessons from engineers, scientists, stewards, and architects who design, operate, and scale real-world data systems. Expect deep, operational conversations about the challenges, trade-offs, and best practices that turn data into repeatable, observable deliverables.

 What we’ll dig into

We will cover topics like your data platform architecture, data engineering at scale, (master) data quality and observability, data modelling from data practitioners and partners.

  • Governance and security: privacy-preserving analytics, metadata management, access policy enforcement, cataloging, encryption, and auditability in regulated environments.
  • Organizational and delivery practices: cross-functional workflows between platform, product, and analytics teams, prioritization under competing SLAs, and techniques for reducing friction between ops and research.

Here is a look at the first episodes

  • A Day in the Life of a Data Steward — Behind the Scenes of Trusted Data

What does a day in the life of a data steward really look like? This podcast pulls back the curtain on the people who keep enterprise data trustworthy, usable, and compliant. We will discuss how these data steward navigate through the rhythms of their day—triaging data incidents, curating metadata, negotiating business definitions, enforcing access controls, and coordinating fixes across engineering, analytics, and business teams. We explore both process realities and the best practices in place to ensure trust.

  • All in on AI: SAP BDC and SAP Business AI - coming soon

Leona Hasani is an SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) expert who specializes in all things data analysis and data management for BDC. In this podcast, she will dive deep into how BDC can help a data scientist leverage all the BDC features and technologies to maximize the value. If you are a data scientist, work with one, or in general, manage some form of the data environment in your team, then this podcast will help you understand all the tools available to you when using SAP Business Data Cloud.

Join us!

If you build data pipelines, ship analytics products, steward sensitive data, or operate ML systems, this season is engineered to deliver actionable guidance you can adapt to your stack and scale.

Subscribe to follow Season 10 (on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, YouTube, and more) and continue the conversation with the Data Professionals Community

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors 1H 2026 Release: What’s New & What Matters
entry.content:
SAP SuccessFactors 1H 2026 Release: What’s New & What Matters

The SAP SuccessFactors 1H 2026 release is not about one big headline feature.

It is about a clear direction across the suite: stronger platform governance, more usable skills intelligence, continued movement to latest experiences, cleaner process handling, and a more connected recruiting story with SmartRecruiters now firmly in the picture.

Like my previous release blog, I am not trying to list every single line item. I am focusing on the updates that actually tell us where the suite is going and what customers, consultants, and HRIT teams should pay attention to first.

Key themes in the 1H 2026 release

  • Platform keeps moving to Admin Center
    Provisioning clean-up continues, more visibility is coming directly into Admin Center, and platform governance is becoming easier to manage.
  • Talent Intelligence is moving from concept to control
    Skills Governance, enhanced people search, target criteria for attribute types and tags, and broader use of custom attributes all point to a more operational skills model.
  • Latest experience continues to become the standard
    Performance, Home Page, and Full Profile updates continue SAP’s steady UX modernization across the suite.
  • Core HR processes are getting tighter
    Employee Central process handling, position follow-up, onboarding document flow recovery, and offboarding dashboard improvements all show a focus on cleaner execution.
  • Recruiting direction is broader than Recruiting Management alone
    Recruiting gets practical usability improvements, while SmartRecruiters for SAP SuccessFactors adds a much bigger strategic story around connected hiring.

 

Platform and administration: one of the biggest stories in 1H 2026

1) Provisioning to Admin Center is not a side note anymore

  • The 1H 2026 batch of Provisioning capabilities is being removed from Provisioning.
  • The direction is clear: more day-to-day administration is expected to happen in Admin Center.
  • Release Center now adds a more visible “What’s New” experience inside Admin Center.

Why it matters

  • Less dependency on legacy admin habits
  • More transparency for customer admins
  • A better long-term operating model for support and governance

2) Admin access and audit clarity are improving

  • The new Manage Super Admin Access tool adds cleaner control over elevated access.
  • The Changed By User fields in the RBP User Role Change Report are now more consistent and easier to interpret.
  • Two new audit data types Document Activities and Media Activities are introduced in General Audit.

Why it matters

  • Admin access management is getting cleaner
  • Platform governance is becoming easier to explain and defend
  • Audit conversations get less confusing

3) Enhanced People Search is one of the most practical Talent Intelligence updates

  • Enhanced People Search continues to improve in 1H 2026.
  • Custom attributes are also becoming more usable in search and matching scenarios.

Why it matters

  • Skills become easier to discover in daily workflows
  • Talent Intelligence becomes more usable, not just more visible

Employee Central and People Profile

A few updates here are more important than they may first look.

  • Personal Quick Links on the latest Home Page improve day-to-day usability.
  • Enhancements in Employment continue strengthening core HR process handling.
  • Position follow-up processes after Job Information imports and API calls are becoming cleaner and more event-driven.
  • Full Profile continues to improve through data viewing, history, and editing enhancements.
  • Support for HRIS Field in Configuration Transport Center, HRIS element in Configuration Transport Center to select fields for transport, with dependency checks, conflict prevention, and detailed import history at the field level.

Why it matters

  • Better employee and admin usability
  • Cleaner handling of core lifecycle events
  • More confidence in data movement and downstream consistency in EC-heavy landscapes

Talent Intelligence Hub, Opportunity Marketplace, and Career growth

This is one of the clearest release stories in 1H 2026.

  • Skills Governance gives a structured process to create and maintain skills with standard naming.
  • Learning recommendations based on skills and attributes are now available in Opportunity Marketplace, Growth Portfolio, and the latest Career Worksheet.
  • Users can now search job roles by custom attributes in Opportunity Marketplace.
  • SAP also introduced target criteria for attribute types and tags.
  • Role and development experiences continue becoming more skills-aware.

Why it matters

  • Skills are becoming operational
  • Growth Portfolio and Opportunity Marketplace are becoming more useful in practice
  • The suite is moving toward a more connected model for learning, mobility, and development

Recruiting

Recruiting in 1H 2026 is more about practical refinement than dramatic reinvention.

  • Active Applicants Filter in Applicant Workbench
  • Reportable custom fields for recruiting templates directly in Admin Center
  • Better hygiene and manageability around recruiting data and admin configuration

Why it matters

  • Recruiters get cleaner workbench behavior
  • Administrators get better reporting flexibility
  • Long-running recruiting landscapes become easier to manage over time

SmartRecruiters for SAP SuccessFactors

This deserves its own section because it is now part of the bigger 1H 2026 story.

  • SmartRecruiters is now integrated with SAP SuccessFactors.
  • The combined experience is positioned around single login, unified navigation, and aligned data.
  • Core organizational data such as job families, cost centers, and locations can flow from SuccessFactors into SmartRecruiters.
  • Customers using SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting are not being forced to migrate.

Why it matters

  • This is SAP’s broader recruiting direction now
  • Hiring is being tied more tightly to core HR, workforce data, and long-term talent architecture
  • The story is no longer only about classic Recruiting Management. It is about connected hiring across the suite

Onboarding and Offboarding

This area includes both practical improvements and one major deadline.

  • Onboarding 1.0 reaches full deprecation with 1H 2026.
  • The enhanced Offboarding Dashboard is automatically enabled when prerequisites are met.
  • SAP now allows users to restart only the e-signature step in document flows after new-hire data changes.

Why it matters

  • Customers still carrying old onboarding assumptions now have a real deadline
  • Offboarding becomes more usable by default
  • Document flow recovery gets more practical and less disruptive

Learning, Performance, and development experience

There is a clear experience and skills pattern here as well.

  • The latest experience of Performance Management forms continues to expand.
  • SAP added further UX enhancements to the latest form experience.
  • SAP introduced the ability to evaluate next-role skills and competencies in the latest Performance Management forms.
  • Learning recommendations are now more tightly tied to skills and attributes across growth experiences.
  • Ability for managers see the alternate manager or HR business partner roles as additional tabs in the Team View page

Why it matters

  • Performance UX is moving toward a cleaner standard
  • Skills are becoming more visible inside development and review processes
  • Learning is being pulled closer to actual talent growth, not just catalog consumption

Compensation, Succession, and transport/governance improvements

This may not be the loudest part of the release, but it is still worth noting.

  • SAP expanded Configuration Transport Center coverage with more Benefits and Succession configurations.
  • Mass Recall Statements by File Upload
  • The redesigned History UI and Editing UI in Full Profile continue expanding to areas including Compensation Information.

Why it matters

  • Better transport coverage helps governance and controlled change movement
  • The broader experience modernization also benefits areas beyond only core profile viewing

People Intelligence and analytics

This is another area where the release direction matters more than any one isolated feature.

  • SAP added new insights and data products for People Intelligence, including new insights for Onboarding and Time and new pages in Recruiting Insights and Learning Insights.
  • SAP also introduced EU Pay Transparency Insights within People Intelligence in SAP Business Data Cloud.
  • Availability of Geofence Data in Story Reports for Clock In Clock Out Time Events
  • Run-time warnings alert you when Table reports may extract large datasets, helping you avoid failures and system impact

Why it matters

  • Analytics is moving closer to business action
  • The suite is becoming stronger at connecting HR processes, skills, and decision support
  • This is where SuccessFactors becomes more strategic for leadership conversations, not just operational reporting

Deprecations and End-of-Maintenance: what customers should not ignore

Every major release has enhancement stories, but 1H 2026 also includes a few deprecations and end-of-maintenance milestones that deserve attention. These are the items customers should actively plan around rather than discover too late.

  • Onboarding 1.0 reaches end of maintenance and full deprecation in 1H 2026
    This is one of the biggest deadlines in the release. Customers still relying on Onboarding 1.0 need to complete their move to the latest Onboarding solution.
  • The 1H 2026 batch of Provisioning capabilities is deleted from Provisioning
    SAP continues removing capabilities from Provisioning where equivalent functionality already exists in Admin Center. This is another clear signal that the long-term administration model is shifting.
  • Cross-System Workflow for SAP SuccessFactors Solutions reaches end of maintenance in 2026
    This is another item customers should review early, especially if they still depend on older workflow patterns across connected systems.
  • Student Person Type is being permanently deleted
    Customers using older contingent or student-related person type assumptions should validate whether this change has any downstream impact in their design.

Why it matters

  • Some 1H 2026 changes are not just enhancements. They require planning and remediation.
  • Older onboarding, workflow, and platform assumptions are steadily being closed out.
  • This release is another reminder to review technical debt, legacy dependencies, and outdated admin practices before they become production issues.

Final thought

If I had to sum up the SAP SuccessFactors H1 2026 release in one line, I would say this:

1H 2026 is about making the suite more governed, more connected, and more usable in real operations.

That is the bigger story behind this release:

  • more Admin Center ownership
  • stronger Talent Intelligence foundations
  • better usability in core experiences
  • cleaner process handling across HR and onboarding
  • a broader recruiting future with SmartRecruiters now clearly part of the suite story

For full technical documentation, visit the SAP SuccessFactors What’s New Viewer.

If you enjoyed this blog, a kudo would mean a lot 😊

entry.title: Technical SUSER password
entry.content:
Hi, our company setup SPHONE_NUMBER as the SAP support backbone user.

If the email assigned is no longer valid, How can we change or know the password?

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Technical SUSER password
entry.content:
Dear customer,

The email address of the technical communication use cannot be changed once the technical user is created.

Actually there is no way to change any of the technical user details (description, e-mail, department, etc).  

However, even though an e-mail address is required to be entered when creating the tech-user, the e-mail address is not used and can be ignored after that. Therefore, if the e-mail address for the user is not correct, there is no need to change it.

If you do want to change the technical user data, a new technical user has to be created.

This is documented in KBA 2438876 - How to change contact details (including e-mail) for a Technical Communication User - SAP for Me.

To reset the technical user password, pls refer to KBA 2393376 - How to reset the password for a Technical Communication User - SAP for Me.

Hope the above information helps. 

Thanks and best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Event-driven architecture
entry.content:
We are currently working on moving a couple of backend processes to event-driven architecture using SAP Business Technology Platform.
In our S/4 system, we already have business events being triggered, but we are not fully clear on the best way to structure the consumer side in BTP.

Right now, we are evaluating both Event Mesh and CAP-based subscribers. My confusion is mainly around when to use a lightweight CAP service vs when Event Mesh alone is sufficient for processing.

Has anyone implemented a similar pattern in production where multiple downstream systems consume the same event? How did you avoid duplication and keep it scalable?

entry.title: SAP Business Technology Platform API Management layer.
entry.content:
We are exposing several S/4HANA services to external applications via SAP Business Technology Platform API Management layer.

One open question we have is around governance vs performance. Some teams are suggesting we bypass API Management for internal apps to reduce latency, while others strongly recommend keeping everything routed through API Management for consistency.

What is the recommended real-world approach here? Do companies actually mix both approaches, or is it better to enforce a single standard?

entry.title: CAP vs RAP confusion in real projects
entry.content:
We are starting a new extension project and trying to decide between CAP model and RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) on SAP Business Technology Platform.

On paper, RAP feels closer to S/4 and CAP feels more flexible, but in real implementations we are not sure where the boundary really lies.

For teams that have used both, how do you decide which one to pick? Is RAP mainly for ABAP-heavy teams and CAP for full cloud-native extensions, or is there more nuance based on use case complexity?

entry.title: sap data services connect starrocks issue
entry.content:
Hello experts:

I need to use  data services to extract data from starrocks database, which is compatiable with mysql. then when creating  the datastore, I chose the mysql type, but it prompted an error :

WARNING: Cannot establish database connection.SQL submitted to database <information_schema> resulted in error<[MySQL][ODBC 8.0(w) Driver][mysqld-8.0.33]Getting analyzing error.Detail message: Set statement only support constant expr..>. The SQLsubmitted is <
SET SESSION sql_mode=(SELECT CONCAT(@@sql_mode,',ANSI_QUOTES'))
>.' (BODI-1111341)

it seems that the starrocks does not support the subquery to assign value.  but the connection is almost successful except the initial submitted sql statement.

I've tried serveral ways to fix it, as following:

1.  change the database type to odbc

2.  input something in the additional session parameters under "advanced"

3. tried to use data services 4.3 to establish the datastore

But so far no progress. it seems that the data services need to sent the initial statement SET SESSION sql_mode=(SELECT CONCAT(@@sql_mode,',ANSI_QUOTES')) ,  don't know how to skip this step.

Could anyone help? Thanks.

 

Allen
